title: Coordinate Geometry
blurb: Master the coordinate plane: points, distance, slope, line equations, intercepts, and parabolas.
theory:
The Coordinate Plane

The coordinate plane is formed by two perpendicular number lines: the horizontal x-axis and the vertical y-axis. They meet at the origin (0, 0). Every point is named by an ordered pair (x, y), where x is the horizontal position and y is the vertical position.

The axes split the plane into four quadrants, numbered counterclockwise starting from the top right:

Quadrantx signy sign
I++
II−+
III−−
IV+−

Points on an axis belong to no quadrant. For example, (0, 5) lies on the y-axis and (−3, 0) lies on the x-axis.

Plotting Points

To plot (4, −2): start at the origin, move 4 right (positive x), then 2 down (negative y). To plot (−3, 1): move 3 left, then 1 up. Always read x first, then y.

The Distance Formula

The distance between (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) comes straight from the Pythagorean theorem:

d = √((x2 − x1)2 + (y2 − y1)2)

Example: distance from (1, 2) to (4, 6) is √((4−1)2 + (6−2)2) = √(9 + 16) = √25 = 5. The horizontal change is 3, the vertical change is 4, giving the classic 3-4-5 right triangle.

The Midpoint Formula

The midpoint of the segment joining (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is just the average of the coordinates:

M = ((x1 + x2)/2, (y1 + y2)/2)

Example: midpoint of (−2, 5) and (6, −1) is ((−2+6)/2, (5+(−1))/2) = (2, 2).

Slope

Slope measures steepness: rise over run. For two points,

m = (y2 − y1) / (x2 − x1)

Interpret the sign and value:

  • Positive slope: line rises left to right (/).
  • Negative slope: line falls left to right (\).
  • Zero slope: horizontal line, equation y = constant.
  • Undefined slope: vertical line, equation x = constant. (Division by zero.)

The larger the absolute value of m, the steeper the line.

Slope-Intercept Form

The most useful form of a line is y = mx + b, where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept (the y-value where the line crosses the y-axis, i.e., where x = 0). For y = 3x − 2, the slope is 3 and the line crosses the y-axis at (0, −2).

Point-Slope Form

If you know one point (x1, y1) and the slope m, use:

y − y1 = m(x − x1)

Example: line through (1, 4) with slope −2 is y − 4 = −2(x − 1), which simplifies to y = −2x + 6.

Finding a Line Equation

Given two points, first compute the slope, then plug one point into point-slope form. Through (2, 3) and (6, 11): slope = (11−3)/(6−2) = 8/4 = 2, so y − 3 = 2(x − 2), giving y = 2x − 1.

x- and y-Intercepts

  • y-intercept: set x = 0 and solve for y. This is b in y = mx + b.
  • x-intercept: set y = 0 and solve for x.

For y = 2x − 6: y-intercept is (0, −6); x-intercept is found by 0 = 2x − 6, so x = 3, giving (3, 0).

Parallel and Perpendicular Lines

  • Parallel lines have equal slopes (m1 = m2) and never intersect.
  • Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals: m1 × m2 = −1. If one slope is 3, the perpendicular slope is −1/3. If one is −2/5, the other is 5/2.

A horizontal line (slope 0) is perpendicular to a vertical line (undefined slope) — this is the one case the negative-reciprocal rule cannot express directly.

Graphs of Lines

To graph y = mx + b: plot the y-intercept (0, b), then use the slope as rise/run to find a second point. For y = (2/3)x + 1, start at (0, 1), go up 2 and right 3 to reach (3, 3).

Basic Parabolas and Shifts

The graph of y = x2 is a U-shaped parabola with vertex at the origin, opening upward and symmetric about the y-axis. Transformations shift or flip it:

EquationEffect
y = x2 + kshifts up by k (down if k is negative)
y = (x − h)2shifts right by h (left if h is negative)
y = (x − h)2 + kvertex at (h, k)
y = −x2opens downward (reflected over x-axis)
y = ax2narrower if |a| > 1, wider if |a| < 1

Note the sign trick: (x − 3)2 shifts right 3, while (x + 3)2 shifts left 3.

Reflections Across the Axes

  • Reflect across the x-axis: (x, y) becomes (x, −y). Negate the y-coordinate.
  • Reflect across the y-axis: (x, y) becomes (−x, y). Negate the x-coordinate.
  • Reflect across the origin: (x, y) becomes (−x, −y). Negate both.

Example: reflecting (4, −7) across the x-axis gives (4, 7); across the y-axis gives (−4, −7).
whatEtsTests:
• Computing slope from two points and matching it to a graph's appearance (rising, falling, steep, flat).
  • Using y = mx + b to identify slope and intercepts, or to determine whether a given point lies on a line.
  • Distance and midpoint formulas, often disguised inside geometry figures or with the answer being a clean Pythagorean triple (3-4-5, 5-12-13).
  • Parallel (equal slopes) and perpendicular (negative-reciprocal slopes) relationships.
  • Identifying which quadrant a point lies in, especially when variables have unknown signs (a frequent cannot-be-determined trap in QC).
  • Reflections across axes and simple parabola shifts (vertex location, direction of opening).
  • Quantitative Comparison items that pit one slope, distance, or coordinate against another.
tips: Recognize Pythagorean triples (3-4-5, 5-12-13, 8-15-17) in the distance formula to skip the arithmetic.
Read slope directly off y = mx + b — the coefficient of x is m, the constant is the y-intercept.
Midpoint is just the average of coordinates; to find a missing endpoint, use endpoint = 2(midpoint) − known endpoint.
For perpendicular slopes, flip the fraction and switch the sign: 3 becomes −1/3, −2/3 becomes 3/2.
Parabola shifts read backward inside the parentheses: (x − h) moves right by h, (x + h) moves left by h.
When a point's coordinates depend on unknown-sign variables, suspect 'cannot be determined' on QC quadrant questions.
traps: Reversing the slope subtraction order — be consistent: (y2 − y1) over (x2 − x1), not mixing x's and y's.
Confusing zero slope (horizontal) with undefined slope (vertical); y = 4 is flat, x = 4 is vertical.
Forgetting the negative sign when taking the perpendicular reciprocal (using 1/3 instead of −1/3).
Misreading parabola shift direction: assuming (x − 3)^2 moves left instead of right.
Dropping a negative when squaring coordinate differences — the result must be positive before adding.

examples.q: What is the distance between the points (−3, 4) and (2, −8)?
examples.steps:
1. Apply the distance formula: d = √((x2 − x1)2 + (y2 − y1)2).
  2. Horizontal change: 2 − (−3) = 5. Vertical change: −8 − 4 = −12.
  3. d = √(52 + (−12)2) = √(25 + 144) = √169 = 13.
examples.shortcut: The legs are 5 and 12 — recognize the 5-12-13 triple and write 13 immediately.

examples.q: A line passes through (2, 3) and (6, 11). What is its equation in slope-intercept form?
examples.steps:
1. Slope m = (11 − 3)/(6 − 2) = 8/4 = 2.
  2. Use point-slope with (2, 3): y − 3 = 2(x − 2).
  3. Distribute: y − 3 = 2x − 4, so y = 2x − 1.
examples.shortcut: With slope 2 and point (2, 3): b = y − mx = 3 − 2(2) = −1, so y = 2x − 1 directly.

examples.q: Line L has equation y = 3x − 5. What is the slope of any line perpendicular to L?
examples.steps:
1. The slope of L is the coefficient of x, which is 3.
  2. Perpendicular slope is the negative reciprocal: flip 3 to 1/3, then negate.
  3. The perpendicular slope is −1/3.

examples.q: The parabola y = (x − 4)2 + 1 has its vertex at which point, and does it open up or down?
examples.steps:
1. Compare to vertex form y = (x − h)2 + k, where the vertex is (h, k).
  2. Here h = 4 and k = 1, so the vertex is (4, 1).
  3. The coefficient of the squared term is positive (+1), so the parabola opens upward.
examples.shortcut: Inside the parentheses the sign flips: (x − 4) means shift right 4; +1 means shift up 1 → vertex (4, 1).

questions.q: In which quadrant does the point (−5, 3) lie?
questions.explain: The x-coordinate is negative and the y-coordinate is positive. Negative x with positive y places the point in the upper-left region, which is Quadrant II.

questions.q: What is the y-intercept of the line y = −4x + 7?
questions.explain: In y = mx + b, the constant b is the y-intercept. Here b = 7, so the line crosses the y-axis at (0, 7).

questions.q: What is the slope of the line passing through (1, 2) and (5, 14)? Enter your answer as a number.
What is the midpoint of the segment joining (−2, 5) and (6, −1)?
questions.explain: Average the coordinates: x = (−2 + 6)/2 = 2, y = (5 + (−1))/2 = 2. The midpoint is (2, 2).

questions.q: A line is perpendicular to the line 2x + 3y = 12. What is the slope of the perpendicular line?
questions.explain: Rewrite in slope-intercept form: 3y = −2x + 12, so y = (−2/3)x + 4. The given slope is −2/3. The perpendicular slope is the negative reciprocal: flip to −3/2 then negate to get 3/2.

questions.q: The point M = (4, 4) is the midpoint of segment AB. If A = (1, 2), what are the coordinates of B?
Line k passes through (0, 0) and (3, 6). Line j passes through (1, 1) and (4, 7).
questions.info: Line k passes through (0, 0) and (3, 6). Line j passes through (1, 1) and (4, 7).
questions.explain: Slope of k = (6 − 0)/(3 − 0) = 2. Slope of j = (7 − 1)/(4 − 1) = 6/3 = 2. Both slopes equal 2, so the quantities are equal (choice C).

questions.q: The parabola y = x2 − 9 crosses the x-axis at which two points?
questions.explain: x-intercepts occur where y = 0: 0 = x2 − 9, so x2 = 9 and x = ±3. The points are (3, 0) and (−3, 0).

questions.q: What is the distance from the origin to the point (8, 15)? Enter your answer as a number.
In the xy-plane, the point P = (a, b) satisfies ab < 0.
questions.info: In the xy-plane, the point P = (a, b) satisfies ab < 0.
questions.qa: The number of the quadrant containing P
questions.explain: ab < 0 means a and b have opposite signs. If a > 0 and b < 0, P is in Quadrant IV (number 4 > 3). If a < 0 and b > 0, P is in Quadrant II (number 2 < 3). Since the quadrant number could be greater than or less than 3, the relationship cannot be determined (choice D).

questions.q: Triangle PQR has vertices P = (0, 0), Q = (6, 0), and R = (6, 8). What is the perimeter of triangle PQR?
questions.explain: PQ is horizontal: length 6. QR is vertical: length 8. PR is the hypotenuse: √(62 + 82) = √100 = 10 (a 6-8-10 triple). Perimeter = 6 + 8 + 10 = 24.

questions.q: Point A = (3, −5) is reflected across the x-axis to give point A′. Which of the following statements are true? Select all that apply.
choices: The distance between A and A′ is 10
The midpoint of segment AA′ is (3, 0)
questions.explain: Reflecting across the x-axis negates the y-coordinate: A′ = (3, 5), so the first statement is true and the fifth is false. A′ has positive x and positive y, so it is in Quadrant I (third statement true). Distance between (3, −5) and (3, 5) is |5 − (−5)| = 10 (second statement true). The midpoint is ((3+3)/2, (−5+5)/2) = (3, 0) (fourth statement true). Both points share x = 3, so segment AA′ is vertical (sixth statement true). The correct selections are statements 1, 2, 3, 4, and 6.
